risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The report states that children were "randomized to one of two maintenance treatment strategies" and that "half of the children [were] randomly assigned to a concurrent 14-day course of oral ciprofloxacin ... and half to oral placebo." Although the exact randomization algorithm is not described in this excerpt, the language indicates a genuine random allocation process rather than a predictable or rule-based method, and there is no evidence of a non-random sequence.

Summary: The report states that participants were "randomized" and that half received ciprofloxacin vs placebo, but it does not describe any mechanism for concealing the next assignment from the recruiters/enrollers. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, identical containers, or any other concealment method, so allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk. Comment: Randomization is described, but concealment from foreknowledge is not.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "During the first quarter of the study, all children received initial Pa eradication treatment consisting of 28–56 days of open label tobramycin inhalation solution (TIS)... with half of the children randomly assigned to a concurrent 14-day course of oral ciprofloxacin ... and half to oral placebo." Comment: Participants and treatment personnel were not fully blinded because the inhaled tobramycin was explicitly open-label. Although the oral ciprofloxacin/placebo component was randomized, knowledge that all children received open-label active inhaled therapy means blinding was incomplete; given the study outcomes included pulmonary exacerbation and antibiotic treatment decisions, this could plausibly influence care delivery and outcome assessment overall.

Quote: "All cultures were processed at the Center for CF Microbiology at Seattle Children’s Hospital... Assessment for a pulmonary exacerbation was done at all scheduled study visits as well as acute illness visits. An a priori, established definition of pulmonary exacerbation was used for the determination of these events." Comment: The microbiologic outcome (P. aeruginosa culture status) was assessed by a centralized lab, which is an objective measurement unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation. The exacerbation outcome used an a priori definition, reducing subjective judgment. The report does not state explicit assessor blinding, but the outcomes described here are objective or protocol-defined and therefore unlikely to be biased by lack of blinding.

Quote: "A total of 282/304 (93%) participants in the EPIC clinical trial were included in the study cohort, based on the availability of the initial eradication outcome at week 10. The 22 excluded children were similar to those included in the current analysis except for a slightly younger age and lower BMI" Comment: The analysed cohort excludes 22/304 participants (~7.2% overall loss) for the week-10 eradication outcome, which is not enough by itself for high risk. However, the study then reports subsequent outcome information only for subsets of the 282 participants (e.g. "A total of 44/282 (16%) participants met... pulmonary exacerbation treated with intravenous antibiotics" and "139/282 (49%) met... pulmonary exacerbation treated with antibiotics by any route"), and the paper does not provide a per-arm/per-time-point accounting of missing outcome data for the maintenance-phase exacerbation analyses. Because the outcome analysis relies on time-to-event data over 15 months without a clear breakdown of losses, censoring, or missing outcome status by arm, this is substantial attrition/missing-data uncertainty and the absence of detailed per-arm numbers itself signals poor handling under the review rules.
